blocks.content: Eli made it up next.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Oh, dang.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Oh, dang.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Oh, dang.

He said it three times with increasing sincerity, then pointed at his own house — the blue roof his mama had been meaning to repaint since April. Savannah came up after him and stood very still, and her eyes got a little bright at the corners, because Millhaven from sixty feet looked like something from a picture book, or a memory you hadn't made yet.

The catwalk was narrower than it looked from the ground. When Daisy came up last and stepped onto it, she moved to stand beside Kit and they were closer than they would have been anywhere else — the railing on one side, the tank behind them, not quite enough room to put real space between them.
Neither of them mentioned it.

Millhaven spread out below. The white spire of First Baptist. The Tasty Freeze sign lit even in daylight. The courthouse copper dome. The long line of trees where the creek ran. Open country past everything.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You can see the creek from here.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yeah.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I didn't know the Henderson property was that close to it.
She pointed. The overgrown yard with the rusted truck was visible — a rectangle of tall grass inside the residential grid, looking smaller and more manageable from here.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: It looks different from up here. Smaller.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Everything does.
She glanced at him. He wasn't looking at the Henderson property. He was looking south, at something else.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: What are you looking at?

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: My mom's street. That oak tree — the big one by the Winstons'. That's how far it is.
Daisy found it. The oak was enormous even from here, its canopy a dark round mass against the paler green of surrounding yards. She'd walked under that tree with him probably a hundred times.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I didn't know you could see it from here.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yeah.
He said it quietly. Not the way he usually said things.

Daisy looked at the tree for a moment, and then she looked at him, and he was still looking at the street, and there was something in his face that she almost said something about and then decided not to.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: We should go back down before Eli tries to stand on the railing.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I was not going to stand on the railing.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You were thinking about it.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I was thinking about a lot of things. That was one of them.
Kit looked at the town one more time. The oak tree. The street. The whole small geography of his life, laid out where he could see all of it at once.
Then he turned toward the ladder.
